abstract: Current document parsing methods compete primarily on model architecture innovation, while systematic engineering of training data remains underexplored. Yet SOTA models of different architectures and parameter scales exhibit highly consistent failure patterns on the same set of hard samples, suggesting that the performance bottleneck stems from shared deficiencies in training data rather than architecture itself. Building on this finding, we present \minerupro, which advances the state of the art solely through data engineering and training strategy optimization while keeping the 1.2B-parameter architecture of \mineru completely fixed. At its core is a Data Engine co-designed around coverage, informativeness, and annotation accuracy: Diversity-and-Difficulty-Aware Sampling expands training data from under 10M to 65.5M samples while correcting distribution shift; Cross-Model Consistency Verification leverages output agreement among heterogeneous models to assess sample difficulty and generate reliable annotations; the Judge-and-Refine pipeline improves annotation quality for hard samples through render-then-verify iterative correction. A three-stage progressive training strategy -- large-scale pre-training, hard sample fine-tuning, and GRPO alignment -- sequentially exploits these data at different quality tiers. On the evaluation front, we fix element-matching biases in OmniDocBench~v1.5 and introduce a Hard subset, establishing the more discriminative OmniDocBench~v1.6 protocol. Without any architectural modification, \minerupro achieves 95.69 on OmniDocBench~v1.6, improving over the same-architecture baseline by 2.71 points and surpassing all existing methods including models with over 200$\times$ more parameters.

citations.fullCitation: Junbo Niu, Zheng Liu, Zhuangcheng Gu, Bin Wang, Linke Ouyang, Zhiyuan Zhao, Tao Chu, Tianyao He, Fan Wu, Qintong Zhang, Zhenjiang Jin, Guang Liang, Rui Zhang, Wenzheng Zhang, Yuan Qu, Zhifei Ren, Yuefeng Sun, Yuanhong Zheng, Dongsheng Ma, Zirui Tang, Boyu Niu, Ziyang Miao, Hejun Dong, Siyi Qian, Junyuan Zhang, Jingzhou Chen, Fangdong Wang, Xiaomeng Zhao, Liqun Wei, Wei Li, Shasha Wang, Ruiliang Xu, Yuanyuan Cao, Lu Chen, Qianqian Wu, Huaiyu Gu, Lindong Lu, Keming Wang, Dechen Lin, Guanlin Shen, Xuanhe Zhou, Linfeng Zhang, Yuhang Zang, Xiaoyi Dong, Jiaqi Wang, Bo Zhang, Lei Bai, Pei Chu, Weijia Li, Jiang Wu, Lijun Wu, Zhenxiang Li, Guangyu Wang, Zhongying Tu, Chao Xu, Kai Chen, Yu Qiao, Bowen Zhou, Dahua Lin, Wentao Zhang, and Conghui He. Mineru2.5: A decoupled vision-language model for efficient high-resolution document parsing. arXiv preprint arXiv:2509.22186, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper introduces MinerU2.5, the direct predecessor and baseline model for the work presented. The core contribution of MinerU2.5-Pro is improving upon this model's performance solely through data engineering and training strategies, while keeping the MinerU2.5 architecture entirely fixed.
citations.title: Mineru2.5: A decoupled vision-language model for efficient high-resolution document parsing

citations.fullCitation: Linke Ouyang, Yuan Qu, Hongbin Zhou, Jiawei Zhu, Rui Zhang, Qunshu Lin, Bin Wang, Zhiyuan Zhao, Man Jiang, Xiaomeng Zhao, et al. Omnidocbench: Benchmarking diverse pdf document parsing with comprehensive annotations. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, pages 24838–24848, 2025.
citations.justification: This is the foundational evaluation benchmark that the paper uses and improves upon. A significant contribution of the main paper is the introduction of OmniDocBench v1.6, which corrects biases and adds a 'Hard' subset to this original benchmark to allow for more discriminative evaluation.
citations.title: Omnidocbench: Benchmarking diverse pdf document parsing with comprehensive annotations

citations.fullCitation: Cheng Cui, Ting Sun, Suyin Liang, Tingquan Gao, Zelun Zhang, Jiaxuan Liu, Xueqing Wang, Changda Zhou, Hongen Liu, Manhui Lin, et al. Paddleocr-vl-1.5: Towards a multi-task 0.9 b vlm for robust in-the-wild document parsing. arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.21957, 2026.
citations.justification: This paper presents a key state-of-the-art competitor model that MinerU2.5-Pro is benchmarked against. Furthermore, its data sampling method (UACS) is explicitly contrasted with the main paper's Cross-Model Consistency Verification (CMCV), highlighting the methodological differences in data-centric approaches.
citations.title: Paddleocr-vl-1.5: Towards a multi-task 0.9 b vlm for robust in-the-wild document parsing

citations.fullCitation: Daochen Zha, Zaid Pervaiz Bhat, Kwei-Herng Lai, Fan Yang, and Xia Hu. Data-centric ai: Perspectives and challenges. In Proceedings of the 2023 SIAM international conference on data mining (SDM), pages 945–948. SIAM, 2023.
citations.justification: This citation provides the theoretical background for the paper's core philosophy. The main paper's entire premise is a practical application of the 'Data-Centric AI' paradigm—systematically improving data quality while keeping the model fixed—to the domain of document parsing.
citations.title: Data-centric ai: Perspectives and challenges

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "MinerU2.5-Pro: Pushing the Limits of Data-Centric Document Parsing at Scale."

---

## Research Report: MinerU2.5-Pro: Pushing the Limits of Data-Centric Document Parsing at Scale

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The paper is authored by Bin Wang, Tianyao He, Linke Ouyang, Fan Wu, Zhiyuan Zhao, Tao Chu, Yuan Qu, Zhenjiang Jin, Weijun Zeng, Ziyang Miao, Bangrui Xu, Junbo Niu, Mengzhang Cai, Jiantao Qiu, Qintong Zhang, Dongsheng Ma, Yuefeng Sun, Hejun Dong, Wenzheng Zhang, Jutao Xiao, Jiayong Shi, Pengyu Liao, Xiaomeng Zhao, Liqun Wei, Jing Yu, Jie Yang, Wei Li, Shasha Wang, Qianqian Wu, Xuanhe Zhou, Weijia Li, Zhenxiang Li, Zhongying Tu, Jiang Wu, Lijun Wu, Chao Xu, Kai Chen, Wentao Zhang, Yu Qiao, Bowen Zhou, Dahua Lin, and Conghui He.

The primary affiliation for most authors is the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory. Junbo Niu, Qintong Zhang, Wenzheng Zhang, and Wentao Zhang are also affiliated with Peking University. Xuanhe Zhou is also affiliated with Shanghai Jiao Tong University. Huaping Zhong is affiliated with SenseTime.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Document parsing, the process of converting unstructured documents into structured, machine-readable formats, serves as a foundational component for various artificial intelligence applications, including large language model (LLM) training data pipelines and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems. Historically, document parsing has evolved from pipeline-based methods, which decompose the task into independent subtasks (e.g., layout detection, text recognition), to end-to-end vision-language models (VLMs) and decoupled VLM methods. Current research efforts in VLM-based document parsing have largely focused on architectural innovation and optimizing inference efficiency, leading to a convergence of performance among leading models on standard benchmarks.

This paper identifies a limitation in this architectural-centric approach: despite diverse architectures and parameter scales, state-of-the-art models exhibit consistent failure patterns on specific "hard" samples. This observation suggests that the performance bottleneck may stem from shared deficiencies in training data rather than architectural design itself. This perspective aligns with the broader "Data-Centric AI" paradigm, which advocates for systematic improvements in data quality while keeping models fixed. While data-centric approaches have been explored in areas like vision-language pretraining and LLM fine-tuning, their systematic application to document parsing, specifically optimizing data coverage, informativeness, and annotation accuracy, has been comparatively underexplored. Previous efforts often treat training data as a prerequisite rather than an independent research focus.

Furthermore, the paper notes deficiencies in existing evaluation frameworks, specifically OmniDocBench v1.5. It identifies element-matching biases that introduce scoring artifacts and a lack of challenging "hard" samples, which reduces the discriminative power of the benchmark as models improve.

In this context, MinerU2.5-Pro positions itself as a data-centric initiative. It aims to advance document parsing performance by exclusively focusing on data engineering and training strategy optimization, rather than architectural modifications. This work seeks to complement architectural advancements by demonstrating the performance gains achievable through a systematic approach to data quality, while also contributing an improved evaluation protocol.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The motivation for this research stems from several identified limitations within the current landscape of document parsing:

*   **Performance Bottleneck in Training Data:** A key observation derived from cross-analysis of multiple state-of-the-art (SOTA) document parsing models is that they exhibit highly similar failure modes on the same challenging samples. These systematic errors occur across models with different architectures and parameter scales, suggesting that the primary performance bottleneck is not the model architecture itself, but rather common deficiencies in the training data.
*   **Insufficient Data Coverage:** Existing training datasets, exemplified by MinerU2.5's data of less than 10 million pages, are often concentrated on high-frequency document categories. This leads to a severe underrepresentation of "long-tail" scenarios, such as complex nested tables, dense formula layouts, and unconventional multi-column structures, which are critical for robust real-world performance.
*   **Annotation Quality Paradox:** The samples that offer the highest marginal value for model improvement are often "hard" samples that no mainstream model can consistently parse correctly. For these difficult cases, automatic annotation methods are prone to errors, particularly for complex structural elements like tables and formulas. This annotation noise propagates directly into the model during supervised fine-tuning, limiting performance gains. The challenge is compounded by the fact that simply increasing data volume without addressing these quality issues can amplify existing biases and noise.
*   **Evaluation Framework Limitations:** The prevalent evaluation benchmark, OmniDocBench v1.5, was identified as having two main shortcomings:
    *   **Element-matching Biases:** Its fixed-granularity one-to-one element matching logic can systematically penalize models whose output segmentation or format conventions differ from the ground truth, even if the content is semantically correct. This introduces scoring artifacts that complicate fair cross-system comparison.
    *   **Insufficient Hard Sample Coverage:** The benchmark contains relatively few hard samples, leading to performance convergence among top models and reduced discriminative power.

Based on these motivations, the key objectives of the MinerU2.5-Pro project are:

*   **To Validate Data-Centric Hypothesis:** To demonstrate that systematic data engineering can be the primary driver for advancing document parsing performance, particularly as model architectures mature.
*   **To Develop a Data Engine:** To design and implement a comprehensive Data Engine that systematically co-optimizes training data across three dimensions: coverage, informativeness, and annotation accuracy. This engine aims to expand data volume significantly while improving its quality.
*   **To Introduce a Progressive Training Strategy:** To devise a multi-stage training strategy that effectively leverages the different quality tiers of data produced by the Data Engine, progressing from large-scale foundational training to targeted refinement and metric alignment.
*   **To Establish an Improved Evaluation Protocol:** To upgrade the OmniDocBench benchmark to v1.6 by correcting element-matching biases through Multi-Granularity Adaptive Matching and introducing a dedicated Hard subset for more discriminative evaluation.
*   **To Achieve State-of-the-Art Performance:** To achieve improved document parsing performance on the new evaluation protocol using the existing 1.2B-parameter MinerU2.5 architecture, attributing all gains directly to data-level and training strategy optimizations.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The development of MinerU2.5-Pro is centered on a data-centric approach, featuring a Data Engine, a progressive training strategy, and an upgraded evaluation protocol, all while retaining the fixed 1.2B-parameter architecture of MinerU2.5.

#### 4.1 Data Engine

The Data Engine is designed to co-optimize data coverage, informativeness, and annotation accuracy through four interconnected components:

*   **Diversity-and-Difficulty-Aware Sampling (DDAS):** This component addresses the long-tail distribution problem in document parsing data by jointly optimizing data diversity and difficulty at both page-level and element-level granularities.
    *   **Page-level sampling:** Visual features (ViT-base embeddings) are extracted from a large PDF pool and clustered using K-Means. An initial candidate set is formed by uniform sampling from cluster centers. Cross-Model Consistency Verification (CMCV) is then applied to these candidates to assign difficulty labels. Sampling weights are adjusted based on the difficulty distribution within each cluster, upweighting diverse and difficult clusters while filtering irrelevant content. This expands data to approximately 60 million pages.
    *   **Element-level sampling:** From the page-level candidate set, layout detection models extract specific elements (text, formula, table blocks). Each element type undergoes independent clustering and CMCV-based difficulty assessment.
    *   **Final sampling:** Balanced sampling occurs in the joint cluster-difficulty space for all subtasks. Large clusters are downsampled, small clusters are upsampled, and Medium and Hard samples are upweighted to enhance informativeness, resulting in a Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) training set.
*   **Cross-Model Consistency Verification (CMCV):** This method assesses sample difficulty by leveraging output agreement among multiple heterogeneous SOTA models (MinerU2.5, PaddleOCR-VL, Qwen3-VL-30B). Unlike single-model uncertainty, CMCV distinguishes model-specific weaknesses from universally hard problems.
    *   **Difficulty Tiers:** Samples are classified into three tiers based on pairwise consistency metrics (edit distance for text, TEDS for tables, CDM for formulas):
        *   **Easy:** MinerU2.5’s output is highly consistent with at least one external model, indicating reliable parsing.
        *   **Medium:** External models agree, but MinerU2.5 differs significantly, pinpointing MinerU2.5’s specific capability gaps. External consensus serves as a pseudo-label.
        *   **Hard:** All models show significant disagreement, indicating genuinely difficult samples requiring further refinement.
*   **Judge-and-Refine Annotation Pipeline:** This pipeline improves annotation accuracy for Hard samples. It utilizes a "render-then-verify" iterative correction mechanism to overcome the "confirmation bias" of naive self-reflection in document parsing.
    *   **Render-then-Verify:** For complex structural mappings (L
        A
        T
        E
        X formulas, HTML tables), generated output is rendered into images. Both the original document image and the rendered image are fed as paired inputs to a Judge-Refine model (Qwen3-VL-235B, chosen for its strong multimodal reasoning and independence from CMCV models). This allows the model to visually detect structural errors that are otherwise subtle in text, localizing defects more effectively.
    *   **Iterative Correction:** Multi-round error localization and targeted correction are performed through visual comparison.
*   **Targeted Expert Annotation:** Samples that remain too complex for automatic Judge-and-Refine correction are routed to human annotators. Annotation budget is prioritized based on Judge confidence (high confidence but uncertain refinement cases get top priority) and subtask weakness. Gemini 3 Pro is used for pre-annotation, followed by expert review and automated QA.

The Data Engine ultimately produces approximately 65.5 million Easy and Medium samples (auto-annotated via CMCV) for pre-training, and 192,000 expert-annotated Hard samples for fine-tuning and reinforcement learning.

#### 4.2 Progressive Training Strategy

MinerU2.5-Pro adopts a three-stage progressive training strategy, starting from a MinerU2.5 Stage 0 checkpoint, with all stages using the fixed 1.2B-parameter architecture:

*   **Stage 1: Document Parsing Pre-training:**
    *   **Data:** Approximately 65.5 million Easy and Medium samples (CMCV auto-annotated) covering text, layout, formula, and table recognition, plus image analysis. The subtask ratio is adjusted based on OmniDocBench weights and baseline performance.
    *   **Configuration:** All parameters are trainable with higher learning rates (LM: 1e-3, Vision: 1e-4) for 1 epoch, using a batch size of 256. This stage focuses on building broad foundational capabilities with significantly scaled data.
*   **Stage 2: High-Quality Supervised Fine-Tuning:**
    *   **Data:** 192,000 expert-annotated Hard samples, mixed with replay data from Stage 1 to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Mixing ratios are differentiated by subtask (e.g., layout 6:1, text 1:50) to balance hard sample training with generalization.
    *   **Configuration:** Lower learning rate (5e-5), batch size 128, trained for 1 epoch. This stage refines the model on challenging scenarios.
*   **Stage 3: Reinforcement Learning with GRPO:**
    *   **Objective:** To bridge the gap between token-level cross-entropy loss and sequence/structure-level evaluation metrics.
    *   **Method:** Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is employed. Candidate outputs are sampled, and rewards are computed directly using task-specific metrics (edit distance, CDM, TEDS, category IoU).
    *   **Data:** Generated from Stage 2 model rollouts, filtered to retain a mid-reward range for effective policy gradient. All data is from the high-quality expert-annotated set.
    *   **Configuration:** Learning rate 1e-5, batch size 512, trained for 1 epoch, with 16 rollouts per sample. Techniques like `clip-higher` and dynamic sampling are used for stability.

#### 4.3 OmniDocBench v1.6 Evaluation Protocol

To provide a more robust and discriminative evaluation, OmniDocBench v1.6 introduces two key improvements:

*   **Multi-Granularity Adaptive Matching (MGAM):** This mechanism eliminates element-matching biases by adaptively adjusting segmentation granularity on the prediction side.
    *   **Three-Stage Process:** Given ground truth elements (G) and prediction elements (P), MGAM generates candidate matching solutions: (1) Direct Bipartite Matching between P and G. (2) Prediction Splitting, where each P element is split at line-break delimiters to form a finer-grained set P', followed by bipartite matching between P' and G. (3) Partition Enumeration, exploring all valid ordered partitions of P' elements to cover intermediate granularities, followed by bipartite matching.
    *   **Global Optimum Selection:** The matching with the best aggregate score among the three stages is selected, and task-specific metrics are computed. This process is applied to both formula and dense text elements, ensuring neutrality to output granularity and format.
*   **Hard Subset and Three-Tier Evaluation Protocol:**
    *   **Hard Subset:** A new subset of 296 pages, selected from the most challenging scenarios (complex nested tables, dense formulas, unconventional layouts) identified during Data Engine stratification. These samples are excluded from all MinerU2.5-Pro training stages and are professionally human-annotated.
    *   **Three-Tier Protocol:** Evaluation is performed across Base (1,355 pages, original v1.5), Hard (296 pages, new challenging set), and Full (1,651 pages, union of Base and Hard).

Beyond the core evaluation, the paper also details **extended parsing capabilities**, including image-aware parsing (classifying image regions and extracting structured content), truncated paragraph merging (reassembling semantically continuous paragraphs split by layout), cross-page table merging (detecting and merging table fragments across page breaks), and in-table image detection (identifying and preserving embedded images within tables). These features enhance real-world usability but do not directly influence OmniDocBench scores.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments conducted on OmniDocBench v1.6 validate the effectiveness of the data-centric approach implemented in MinerU2.5-Pro.

#### 5.1 End-to-End Document Parsing

*   **Overall Performance:** MinerU2.5-Pro achieved an overall score of 95.69 on OmniDocBench v1.6 Full, ranking first among all evaluated methods. This represents a 2.71-point improvement over the baseline MinerU2.5 model (92.98), despite maintaining the identical 1.2B-parameter architecture. This result supports the hypothesis that data engineering can significantly drive performance gains.
*   **Performance on Hard Subset:** On the newly introduced Hard subset, MinerU2.5-Pro scored 94.08, surpassing GLM-OCR and PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 (both 92.01) by 2.07 points. This demonstrates the model's enhanced robustness in challenging scenarios and confirms the discriminative power of the Hard subset. While top models on the Base subset were tightly clustered (within 0.5 points), the Hard subset effectively differentiated their capabilities.
*   **Sub-Metrics:** MinerU2.5-Pro achieved the highest scores in formula recognition (CDM 97.29), table recognition (TEDS 93.42, TEDS-S 95.92), and reading order extraction (0.120 Edit Distance). General-purpose VLMs like Gemini 3 Pro/Flash showed competitive results after the OmniDocBench v1.6 matching corrections but were still outperformed by specialized models, including MinerU2.5-Pro.

#### 5.2 Training Stage Ablation

An ablation study on the three-stage progressive training strategy revealed the incremental contributions of each stage:

*   **Stage 1 (Large-Scale SFT):** Contributed the largest single-stage gain of +1.31 overall points. This indicates that the Data Engine's improvements in data coverage and initial annotation quality (via CMCV auto-annotations) are a primary driver of performance.
*   **Stage 2 (Hard-Sample SFT):** Provided an additional +0.96 overall points. This stage was particularly impactful for table recognition, showing a +2.50 improvement in TEDS score (from 90.37 to 92.87), underscoring the value of high-quality expert-annotated hard samples.
*   **Stage 3 (GRPO Alignment):** Contributed +0.45 overall points, mainly observed in formula CDM improvement (+0.81 from 96.48 to 97.29). This demonstrates the effectiveness of reinforcement learning in aligning training objectives directly with task-level evaluation metrics.
*   **Balanced Improvement:** The cumulative improvement on the Hard subset (+2.43 points, from 91.65 to 94.08) was comparable to that on the Base subset (+2.89 points, from 93.23 to 96.12), indicating a balanced capability improvement across both standard and challenging scenarios.

#### 5.3 Element-Specific Parsing

To isolate content recognition capabilities from layout detection errors, element-specific evaluations were conducted on ground-truth cropped regions:

*   **Text Recognition:** MinerU2.5-Pro achieved an Edit Distance of 0.019 on OmniDocBench v1.6 Full, leading the competition and representing a 30.5% reduction from the MinerU2.5 baseline (0.028). Hundred-billion-scale general VLMs showed competitive performance.
*   **Formula Recognition:** MinerU2.5-Pro secured the best CDM scores in 5 out of 9 benchmarks and ranked second in the remaining 4. It achieved 99.20 CDM on OmniDocBench Base, nearing the performance ceiling. While it showed slight deficits in handwritten (HWE) and screen-captured (SCE) formulas against some competitors, it maintained strong performance across diverse formula types.
*   **Table Recognition:** MinerU2.5-Pro ranked first in overall TEDS (91.10) and TEDS-S (94.48), an improvement of 3.16 and 2.31 percentage points over MinerU2.5, respectively. Its advantage was most significant on the Hard subset (TEDS 92.46 vs. MinerU2.5's 88.28, a +4.18 improvement), highlighting the impact of hard sample mining and annotation on table recognition.

#### 5.4 Qualitative Comparisons

Qualitative comparisons demonstrated MinerU2.5-Pro's accuracy on complex document elements:

*   **Tables:** Showed superior accuracy on rotated tables and those with long merged cells, correctly recovering structure and content where other models exhibited errors.
*   **Formulas:** High accuracy on multi-line formulas due to its decoupled row-by-row analysis, outperforming end-to-end approaches, and accurate recognition of complex matrices.
*   **Image-Aware Parsing:** Effectively extracted structured content from chart and diagram regions that competing models typically treated as opaque images.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research contributes to the field of document parsing by demonstrating the efficacy of a data-centric approach in driving performance advancements, even when model architecture remains fixed.

*   **Validation of Data-Centric AI:** The primary significance of MinerU2.5-Pro is its empirical validation of the hypothesis that systematic data engineering can be a more impactful lever for performance improvement than architectural innovation, particularly as model architectures mature. This provides an orthogonal pathway for research and development in document parsing.
*   **Enhanced Document Parsing Capabilities:** MinerU2.5-Pro establishes a new state of the art in document parsing, exhibiting enhanced robustness, especially on challenging and "hard" document samples. This directly translates to more reliable and accurate extraction of structured information from diverse documents, which is crucial for downstream applications like training large language models and improving retrieval-augmented generation systems.
*   **Systematic Data Engineering Framework:** The developed Data Engine, with its components like Diversity-and-Difficulty-Aware Sampling, Cross-Model Consistency Verification, and the Judge-and-Refine pipeline, provides a systematic and scalable methodology for constructing high-quality, diverse, and informative training datasets. This framework offers practical tools for the community to improve data quality efficiently, including targeted expert annotation for the most difficult cases.
*   **Improved Training Strategies:** The three-stage progressive training strategy effectively leverages different tiers of data quality, moving from broad foundational training to targeted refinement and metric-aligned optimization. This demonstrates a principled approach to combining large-scale noisy data with smaller, high-quality, difficult samples.
*   **Refined Evaluation Standards:** OmniDocBench v1.6, with its Multi-Granularity Adaptive Matching and dedicated Hard subset, establishes a fairer, more discriminative, and comprehensive evaluation protocol. This addresses previous biases and provides a more robust benchmark for assessing the true capabilities of document parsing models, particularly on challenging real-world documents.
*   **Extended Functionality:** The inclusion of extended parsing capabilities, such as image-aware parsing, truncated paragraph merging, cross-page table merging, and in-table image detection, enhances the practical usability and completeness of document understanding in complex real-world scenarios.

**Potential Future Directions:** The paper also acknowledges ongoing challenges. Fundamental limitations in evaluation, such as semantic-equivalence-aware metrics for ambiguous content representations and structural interpretations, remain open research problems. The need for domain-specific evaluation sets for vertical industries (finance, legal, medical) and ensuring the precision of evaluation annotations themselves as models approach human-level performance are noted. Finally, advancing document parsing from purely "content extraction" to "structured semantic understanding," including hierarchical relationships, semantic bindings between elements, and cross-page continuity, represents a logical next step for the field.
overview:
## The Data-Centric Evolution of Document Parsing

Document parsing—the process of converting unstructured PDF and image-based documents into structured, machine-readable formats like Markdown or HTML—is a critical component of modern AI data pipelines. It serves as the primary gateway for feeding high-quality information into Large Language Models (LLMs) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. While initial efforts focused on complex multi-step pipelines and vision-language model (VLM) architectures, recent research suggests that architectural innovations alone are reaching a point of diminishing returns.

MinerU2.5-Pro represents a shift toward a data-centric methodology. The core hypothesis of this work is that common failure modes across different state-of-the-art models are not the result of architectural flaws, but rather shared deficiencies in training data quality and coverage. By maintaining the fixed $1.2\text{B}$ architecture of the previous MinerU2.5 and focusing exclusively on systematic data engineering, the researchers aimed to push the limits of what a smaller-scale model can achieve.

![MinerU2.5-Pro Performance Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04771v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Comprehensive benchmark ranking on OmniDocBench v1.6, demonstrating the performance of MinerU2.5-Pro across Full, Base, and Hard document subsets.*

This approach addresses the "long-tail" problem in document understanding: the difficulty of parsing complex nested tables, dense mathematical formulas, and unconventional multi-column layouts that appear infrequently in standard datasets but are essential for robust real-world performance.

## The MinerU2.5-Pro Data Engine: Strategy Over Architecture

The centerpiece of this work is the "Data Engine," a framework designed to co-optimize data coverage, informativeness, and annotation accuracy. Instead of merely increasing the volume of training data, the engine uses visual and model-based heuristics to select and refine the most valuable samples.

![Data Engine Workflow](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04771v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The architecture of the MinerU2.5-Pro Data Engine, illustrating the path from a raw PDF pool to high-quality training samples.*

The engine's first component is Diversity-and-Difficulty-Aware Sampling (DDAS). It begins by extracting visual features from millions of PDFs using ViT-base embeddings, which are then grouped through K-Means clustering. This ensures that the sampling process captures a wide variety of document styles rather than over-representing common layouts. From this diverse pool, the system uses a hierarchy of difficulty assessments at both the page and element levels (e.g., text blocks, tables, and formulas).

![Sampling Strategy](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04771v1/x3.png)
*Figure 3: Details of the DDAS mechanism, showing how page-level and element-level clusters are sampled based on difficulty tiers.*

By upweighting "Hard" clusters—those containing complex structural elements—the engine expands the effective training data from a few million pages to approximately 60 million, prioritizing samples that offer the highest marginal learning value.

## Cross-Model Consistency and the Judge-Refine Pipeline

To manage such a massive scale of data without introducing excessive noise, the researchers implemented Cross-Model Consistency Verification (CMCV). This technique identifies sample difficulty by comparing the outputs of multiple heterogeneous models (such as MinerU2.5, PaddleOCR-VL, and Qwen3-VL-30B).

The consistency is categorized into three tiers:
1. **Easy:** High agreement across all models; these are used for foundational training.
2. **Medium:** External models agree, but the baseline MinerU2.5 differs. Here, the consensus among other models acts as a pseudo-label to correct the baseline model.
3. **Hard:** Significant disagreement across all models, indicating a genuinely difficult case that requires further refinement or expert human intervention.

For the Hard samples, the "Judge-and-Refine" pipeline introduces a "render-then-verify" mechanism. Since complex structural formats like LaTeX or HTML are difficult for models to verify purely through text, the pipeline renders the model's prediction back into an image. A large, sophisticated "Judge" model (Qwen3-VL-235B) then compares the rendered image of the prediction against the original document image. This visual feedback allows the system to localize structural errors—such as missing table rows or misaligned formula symbols—with high precision. Samples that remain ambiguous after this process are routed to human experts, ensuring the final fine-tuning set is of the highest possible quality.

## A Three-Stage Progressive Training Strategy

MinerU2.5-Pro utilizes a multi-stage training pipeline to integrate these different data tiers effectively. Each stage has a specific objective, scaling from broad understanding to fine-grained structural alignment.

### Stage 1: Document Parsing Pre-training
This stage uses approximately 65.5 million Easy and Medium samples. The focus is on building broad foundational capabilities across all subtasks (layout detection, text, formula, and table recognition). In this stage, all parameters are trainable with relatively high learning rates: $\eta_{\text{LM}} = 10^{-3}$ for the language model component and $\eta_{\text{vision}} = 10^{-4}$ for the vision encoder.

### Stage 2: High-Quality Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
The model is then refined on a set of 192,000 expert-annotated Hard samples. To prevent the model from forgetting the broad knowledge gained in Stage 1, "replay data" is mixed in at task-specific ratios. This stage is where the model learns to handle the most complex document structures that typically cause smaller models to fail.

### Stage 3: Reinforcement Learning with GRPO
The final stage employs Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to bridge the gap between standard training losses and task-specific evaluation metrics. Instead of just predicting the next token, the model is rewarded based on sequence-level metrics like $\text{TEDS}$ (for tables) and $\text{CDM}$ (for formulas). By sampling multiple outputs for each prompt and rewarding those that achieve the best structural accuracy, the model learns to align its internal weights with the actual goals of the parsing task.

## Advancing Evaluation: OmniDocBench v1.6

A significant contribution of this work is the upgrade of the evaluation framework to OmniDocBench v1.6. The researchers identified that previous benchmarks suffered from element-matching biases. For instance, if a model's segmentation of a dense text block differed from the ground truth—even if the content was 100% correct—the scoring logic might penalize it heavily due to a lack of a one-to-one match.

![Matching Bias Examples](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04771v1/x4.png)
*Figure 4: Visual examples of element-matching biases where traditional one-to-one matching leads to incorrect scoring artifacts despite correct content recognition.*

To solve this, MinerU2.5-Pro introduces Multi-Granularity Adaptive Matching (MGAM). Given the ground truth set $G = \{g_1, g_2, \dots, g_n\}$ and the prediction set $P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_m\}$, MGAM follows a three-stage adaptive process:
1. **Direct Bipartite Matching:** Standard matching between $P$ and $G$.
2. **Prediction Splitting:** Splitting predicted elements $P$ into a finer-grained set $P'$ to find better matches with $G$.
3. **Partition Enumeration:** Exploring potential groupings of $P'$ elements to find the global optimum that best reflects the semantic correctness of the output.

Furthermore, a new "Hard" subset of 296 professionally annotated pages was added to the benchmark. This subset specifically targets the failure points of current top-tier models, providing a more discriminative test of a model's true robustness.

## Results and Quantitative Analysis

The data-centric approach yielded significant performance gains across all document categories. On the OmniDocBench v1.6 Full dataset, MinerU2.5-Pro achieved an overall score of 95.69, an improvement of 2.71 points over the baseline MinerU2.5, despite having no changes to the model architecture.

The model's performance on the Hard subset was particularly noteworthy, scoring 94.08 and outperforming much larger models like PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 and GLM-OCR. This validates the effectiveness of the targeted hard-sample mining and the iterative refinement pipeline.

In specific sub-metrics, the model showed high precision:
- **Formula Recognition:** Achieved a $\text{CDM}$ score of 97.29, leading across specialized benchmarks.
- **Table Recognition:** Achieved a $\text{TEDS}$ score of 93.42 and a $\text{TEDS-S}$ (structure only) score of 95.92.
- **Text Recognition:** Reduced the $\text{Edit Distance}$ to 0.019, reflecting a 30.5% reduction in errors compared to the previous version.

Ablation studies confirmed that while scaling the data (Stage 1) provided the largest foundational gain, the expert-guided SFT (Stage 2) and the RL-based alignment (Stage 3) were crucial for achieving state-of-the-art results on complex tables and formulas.

![Qualitative Table Parsing](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04771v1/x17.png)
*Figure 5: Qualitative comparison of table parsing. MinerU2.5-Pro demonstrates superior accuracy in handling long merged cells and complex multi-row structures compared to general-purpose VLMs.*

## Beyond Extraction: Extended Parsing Capabilities

The researchers also expanded the model's utility for real-world document workflows through several functional enhancements:
- **Image-Aware Parsing:** The model can classify chart or diagram regions and extract their internal structure as Mermaid code or tables, rather than treating them as opaque images.
- **Truncated Paragraph Merging:** It can identify and reassemble paragraphs that were split across pages or by layout elements like images.
- **Cross-Page Table Merging:** It detects when a single table spans multiple pages and merges the fragments into a unified structured output.
- **In-Table Image Detection:** It can preserve the identity and location of images embedded within table cells, ensuring no information is lost during the conversion to text-based formats.

These features, while not directly measured by standard benchmark scores, represent a move toward holistic document understanding that prioritizes the semantic continuity of the information over simple region-by-region extraction. By focusing on the "what" (data) rather than just the "how" (architecture), MinerU2.5-Pro establishes a new benchmark for efficiency and accuracy in the field of document parsing.

keyInsights: Performance gains in document parsing can be primarily driven by systematic data engineering, even with a fixed model architecture.
Cross-Model Consistency Verification (CMCV) effectively assesses sample difficulty and generates high-quality pseudo-labels by leveraging consensus among multiple heterogeneous models.
The "render-then-verify" iterative correction mechanism in annotation significantly improves accuracy for complex structural elements by visually detecting errors.

A progressive training strategy, combining large-scale diverse data with targeted high-quality hard samples and metric-aligned reinforcement learning, is effective for comprehensive model improvement.
originalProblem: Existing state-of-the-art document parsing models exhibit similar failure patterns on challenging samples, suggesting training data deficiencies as a primary bottleneck.
Current training datasets lack coverage for "long-tail" complex document structures and suffer from annotation noise, particularly for hard samples.
The OmniDocBench v1.5 evaluation benchmark contains element-matching biases and insufficient challenging samples, limiting its discriminative power for advanced models.
results: MinerU2.5-Pro attained an overall score of 95.69 on OmniDocBench v1.6 Full, improving the baseline MinerU2.5 by 2.71 points while maintaining the same 1.2B-parameter architecture.
The model achieved a score of 94.08 on the challenging new Hard subset, demonstrating enhanced robustness and outperforming other leading models by more than 2 points.
Element-specific evaluations showed MinerU2.5-Pro achieved a 0.019 Edit Distance for text recognition (a 30.5% reduction) and top scores in formula (CDM 97.29) and table recognition (TEDS 93.42).
solution: A comprehensive Data Engine was developed to co-optimize training data coverage, informativeness, and annotation accuracy through diversity-aware sampling and cross-model consistency verification.
A Judge-and-Refine annotation pipeline, supported by targeted expert annotation, was implemented to enhance the quality of labels for challenging "hard" samples.
A three-stage progressive training strategy, including large-scale pre-training, high-quality supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, was designed for a fixed 1.2B-parameter model.
The OmniDocBench benchmark was upgraded to v1.6, introducing Multi-Granularity Adaptive Matching and a new Hard subset for more robust and discriminative evaluation.
summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory advanced document parsing performance by developing MinerU2.5-Pro, a data-centric framework that significantly improves training data quality and implements a progressive training strategy. This approach led to a 95.69 overall score on OmniDocBench v1.6 Full, representing a 2.71-point improvement over the baseline model while maintaining the original 1.2B-parameter architecture.

title: MinerU2.5-Pro: Pushing the Limits of Data-Centric Document Parsing at Scale
